title: The House of Barzani
subtitle: How a Mountain Sufi Lodge Became a Kurdish Dynasty

blocks.text: Long before the Barzanis became an influential political household in modern Iraqi Kurdistan, they were sheikhs in a remote mountain village. Their authority began in a small Sufi lodge, among poor peasants, rural communities, disciples, and rival tribes.

Barzan is a small village that lies in the far northwestern corner of Erbil province, close to the borders of both Iran and Turkey. Before the emergence of the Barzani sheikhdom, it was a small village situated within the wider sphere of the Zebari tribe. The ancestors of the Barzanis themselves belonged to this broader Zebari world. Yet over time, the village would become something far more significant than a geographical location. It would lend its name to a religious tradition and eventually a political movement.

The foundations of that transformation were laid in the late eighteenth century by Sheikh Taj al-Din, who established a takya in Barzan and gave the village its first organized religious identity. Leadership subsequently passed through several generations before reaching Sheikh Abdul Rahman, whose adoption of the Naqshbandi order between 1820 and 1825 is generally regarded as the institutional beginning of the Barzani sheikhdom.

Following Abdul Rahman’s death, leadership passed to Sheikh Abdul Salam I and later to his son Sheikh Muhammad. Under Sheikh Muhammad, Barzan's influence expanded beyond purely religious affairs and began to acquire a broader social role. When he died in 1903, succession passed to his son, Sheikh Abdul Salam II, who would become one of the first Barzani leaders to connect the sheikhdom's authority with emerging Kurdish political aspirations. His execution by Ottoman authorities in 1914 marked a turning point. Leadership then moved to his younger brother, Sheikh Ahmed, regarded as the last spiritual leader of Barzan before the religious sheikhdom gradually gave way to more explicitly political forms of leadership.

Barzan has never functioned solely as a tribe in the conventional Kurdish sense and it differed from many neighboring Kurdish religious houses. Unlike the sheikhly families of Nehri or the major Qadiri orders, the sheikhs of Barzan never developed into a wealthy landed aristocracy. Their influence rested primarily on personal charisma, religious prestige, and the devotion of their followers. This distinction would profoundly shape the character of the movement.

In his renowned book The Origin of Barzani Beliefs, Farid Asasard argues that the beliefs that emerged in Barzan drew upon two intertwined traditions: Naqshbandi Sufism and elements of Ismaili thought, both of which emphasized the inner meaning of faith over outward ritual. The community also developed a culture of secrecy, including the practice of taqiyya (concealing one's beliefs when necessary), helping preserve its cohesion in a region dominated by rival tribes, powerful landlords, and competing religious authorities. At the same time, Barzan cultivated a social ethos rooted in Sufi ideals of humility, poverty, and service, attracting poor peasants and marginalized rural communities while distinguishing itself from neighboring religious families that accumulated wealth and land. By the early twentieth century, Barzan had evolved into a disciplined religious and social movement whose leaders believed they had a sacred mission to combat oppression and defend the downtrodden. By the late nineteenth century the Barzanis had become one of the five most influential religious families among the Kurdish speaking people in the region.

Sheikh Muhammad occupies a pivotal place in Barzani history as he bridged the gap between a secluded Sufi sheikhdom and the mass movement that would later shape Kurdish politics. Under his leadership, Barzan expanded beyond a religious community and increasingly attracted poor peasants and marginalized groups who saw in its teachings a challenge to the region's entrenched feudal order. While cautious about openly proclaiming the movement's doctrines, Sheikh Muhammad strengthened its social foundations and broadened its influence across the surrounding countryside. By the time of his death in 1903, Barzan had evolved from a remote spiritual center into a cohesive community with a growing sense of purpose and identity. It was this foundation that his son, Sheikh Abdul Salam II, would inherit and transform into a far more ambitious movement, one that would bring Barzan into direct confrontation with tribal elites, Ottoman authorities, and eventually the politics of Kurdish nationalism.
The Rise and Death of Sheikh Abdul Salam II

When Sheikh Muhammad died, leadership of the Barzan community passed to his son, Sheikh Abdul Salam II. Under him, the movement entered a new phase. The Barzan sheikhdom expanded its reach, challenged established power structures, and began to engage with political questions that extended beyond the boundaries of the village itself.

Born in Barzan in 1887, Abdul Salam (later known as Sheikh Abdul Salam II to distinguish him from his grandfather, Sheikh Abdul Salam) assumed leadership during a period of profound change, as the Ottoman Empire entered its final decades and new political currents began to reshape Kurdish society. Kurdish intellectuals, tribal leaders, and religious figures were increasingly debating the future of their communities within a rapidly changing imperial order.

Abdul Salam remained first and foremost a sheikh. As head of the Barzan lodge, he was expected to guide followers, settle disputes, preserve religious discipline, and provide moral leadership. His authority rested as much on his reputation as a protector of ordinary villagers as on his position within the Naqshbandi tradition.

Abdul Salam was the figure who completed the transformation of Barzan from a religious network into a coherent social and political community. One of his most significant achievements was the creation of a tribal confederation under Barzan's leadership. He united four major tribal groupings - Berroj, Mizuri, Sherwan, and Dolamari - into a single structure that recognized the authority of the Barzan sheikhdom.

This development fundamentally altered the balance of power in the region. As Barzan's influence expanded, it came into direct conflict with the dominant aristocratic families of neighboring tribes, including the Zebari, Bradosti, Rekani, and Surchi. These powerful aghas derived their authority from landownership, hereditary status, and long-established tribal hierarchies.

In his extensive history of the Barzani family, historian Ayoub Barzani (himself a descendant of the Barzanis and cousin and brother-in-law of current KDP leader Masoud Barzani), writes that Abdul Salam devoted much of his time to listening to people's grievances, supporting scholars and religious learning, caring for widows and orphans, and mediating disputes among tribes and families. In Ayoub's portrayal, Abdul Salam's authority rested not only on his position as a religious leader but also on his reputation as a community servant and protector of the vulnerable.

The Young Turk Revolution of 1908 briefly seemed to create new opportunities. Across the Ottoman Empire, constitutional language and promises of reform encouraged various communities to articulate their demands more openly. Kurdish leaders and intellectuals began discussing questions of language, administration, and local governance with renewed confidence.

It was during this period that Abdul Salam became associated with a series of demands that later historians would regard as early expressions of Kurdish political consciousness. British historian and the author of A Modern History of the Kurds David McDowall identifies Sheikh Abdul Salam as one of the earliest Kurdish leaders to advocate measures such as Kurdish-language education, the appointment of Kurdish-speaking officials in Kurdish districts, and greater local self-government. These demands represented a notable development. They introduced a political vocabulary that extended beyond purely religious concerns while remaining firmly rooted within the Ottoman framework.

Abdul Salam's focus remained overwhelmingly local. The reforms he sought were intended primarily for the Kurdish-speaking areas surrounding Barzan and broader Badinan region. His goal was recognition, fair administration, and greater local authority.

This distinction is emphasized by Assasard, who argues that Abdul Salam's movement was not nationalist in the modern sense. It was neither an urban intellectual movement nor a mass nationalist organization. Its foundations remained rural, religious, and social. The concerns that motivated many of its followers were land, justice, taxation, local administration, and protection from exploitation rather than abstract national ideology.

His growing prominence soon brought him into direct confrontation with the Ottoman authorities. In 1909, Mosul governor Muhammad Fazil Pasha launched a military campaign against Barzan. Officially, the operation aimed to restore order and strengthen state authority. For Abdul Salam's followers, however, it was seen as an attempt to dismantle an increasingly autonomous community and destroy the influence of its leader.

The campaign forced Abdul Salam to flee the region. Seeking refuge, he first found protection with Mar Shimun, the Assyrian Patriarch, before later receiving support from Sayyid Taha of Nehri. Although Ottoman forces occupied and damaged Barzan, the campaign failed to eliminate Abdul Salam's influence. Through mediation involving senior officials, he eventually received an amnesty and returned to Barzan in 1910.

The years that followed proved to be the most significant of his leadership. During this period, he expanded his contacts with influential Kurdish figures across the region. Among those associated with him were Sheikh Abdul Qadir Nehri, Abdul Razzaq Bedirkhan, and Simko Shikak. These relationships connected Barzan to broader currents of Kurdish political activity emerging throughout the late Ottoman world.

Abdul Salam also sought contacts beyond the Ottoman Empire. In 1913, he traveled to Tbilisi alongside Simko Shikak, where he met with a representative of the Russian Tsar. (Masoud Barzani stated in 2023 that Russian Foreign Minister Sergei Lavrov had told him that Abdul Salam met not merely with Russian officials but with Tsar Nicholas II himself.) While the precise details of the encounter remain difficult to verify, the journey nonetheless illustrates Abdul Salam's efforts to seek external support at a time when many Kurdish leaders were attempting to navigate the rivalries of the region's great powers and secure backing for their political ambitions.
Sheikh Abdul Salam II (left) with a representative of Tsar Nicholas II in Tbilisi, 1913

His return journey proved disastrous. Ottoman authorities learned of his whereabouts while he was staying in a Kurdish area of Iran. He was arrested and transferred into Ottoman custody. By then, Ottoman officials had become increasingly suspicious of any movement that appeared capable of mobilizing significant local support.
Abdul Salam was taken to Mosul and placed under close scrutiny. On 14 December 1914, shortly after the Ottoman Empire entered the First World War, he was executed alongside several companions.

The execution of Abdul Salam left Barzan facing one of the most uncertain moments in its history. Leadership now passed to his younger brother, Sheikh Ahmed, who was still a relatively young man inheriting a community under pressure from state authorities, rival tribal forces, and the wider instability engulfing the region. Yet while Abdul Salam had expanded Barzan's horizons into the political sphere, Sheikh Ahmed would inherit the deeper challenge of preserving the movement itself. Over the following decades, he would emerge as the last great spiritual leader of Barzan, guiding the community through rebellion, exile, and war while safeguarding the religious and social foundations upon which his predecessors had built their authority.

One of the most distinctive symbols associated with the Barzanis from its early days has been the red-and-white turban. Traditionally, Kurdish turbans are predominantly white with black patterns or featured regional designs that reflected local and tribal identities. The Barzanis' adoption of a red-and-white turban therefore set them apart and quickly became one of the most recognizable markers of the community. The earliest known literary reference to the tradition dates to 1909, suggesting that it was already an established symbol of Barzani identity by the beginning of the twentieth century.

The turban served both practical and symbolic purposes, distinguishing Barzanis from neighboring tribes while reinforcing a shared sense of belonging. Because the Barzan sheikhdom had long been associated with defending the poor, protecting marginalized communities, and challenging local feudal elites, some later observers mistakenly linked the red color to socialist or communist symbolism. In reality, the tradition predated modern ideological movements by decades and emerged from Barzan's own religious, social, and cultural heritage.

When Sheikh Ahmed inherited leadership of the Barzan sheikhdom, he was barely eighteen years old. Unlike his elder brother, Abdul Salam, he did not present himself as a political reformer, but rather as a spiritual and communal leader navigating a period of profound upheaval.

Contemporary accounts and later historians alike portray him primarily as a religious leader whose authority rested upon his role as head of the Barzan Naqshbandi lodge. Assasard argues that Sheikh Ahmed's greatest achievement was neither military nor political. Rather, it was his ability to preserve and strengthen the internal cohesion of Barzan during decades of upheaval. Under his leadership, Barzan evolved from a religious center and tribal confederation into a remarkably disciplined community whose collective identity survived repeated military defeats and exiles.

Abdul Musawwar Barzani, an academic and grandson of Abdul Salam II, presents a similar image, though in more reverential terms. In Protection of the Kurds, Sheikh Ahmed appears as a man of personal austerity and moral authority, deeply committed to the welfare of his followers and largely indifferent to wealth or personal power. He is depicted as a leader who viewed service to the community as a religious duty and who sought to preserve the values inherited from his predecessors.

The challenge facing Sheikh Ahmed was that preserving that way of life increasingly required confronting a new political reality. The Ottoman Empire, with which the Barzanis had maintained a difficult but familiar relationship, disappeared after the First World War. In its place emerged the British-backed Kingdom of Iraq, a state that sought to extend its authority into regions that had historically enjoyed considerable autonomy.

For many communities, these changes represented modernization and state-building. In Barzan, they were often perceived as encroachments upon local autonomy. Sheikh Ahmed viewed these developments primarily through the lens of communal survival rather than political ideology. The issue was not whether Iraq should exist or whether Kurdistan should become independent. The immediate question was whether Barzan could continue to govern its own affairs according to traditions that had evolved over generations.

This distinction would later become one of the central debates surrounding Sheikh Ahmed's legacy. McDowall argues that Sheikh Ahmed's struggles with the Iraqi state were rooted largely in local concerns and traditional resistance to outside interference. Assasard arrives at a similar conclusion, portraying the Barzani movement during this period as a religious-social community defending its autonomy rather than pursuing a modern nationalist programme.

Masoud Barzani, however, offers a broader interpretation. In Barzani and the Kurdish Liberation Movement, he situates Sheikh Ahmed's leadership within the wider Kurdish experience that followed the First World War. The collapse of the Ottoman Empire, the division of Kurdistan among several states, and the failure to fulfill promises made to the Kurds after the war all form part of the backdrop to his narrative. From this perspective, the struggles of Barzan cannot be understood solely as local disputes. They were also manifestations of a larger Kurdish frustration with the political order that emerged after 1918.

By the end of the 1920s, tensions between Barzan and the Iraqi government had become increasingly difficult to contain. Baghdad sought greater control over the northern frontier regions, while Barzan resisted measures that threatened its autonomy. Disputes emerged over taxation, administrative authority, military access, and the establishment of permanent government institutions inside the region.

According to Assasard, one particularly sensitive issue was the state's determination to station armed officials permanently in Barzan. For generations, the Barzanis had resisted such arrangements. Even under Ottoman rule, they had preserved a considerable degree of self-government and had largely prevented outside authorities from maintaining a permanent presence in their territory. To Sheikh Ahmed and his followers, the establishment of police posts and military facilities represented far more than routine administrative reform. It symbolized the erosion of the autonomy that had defined Barzan for decades.

In Masoud Barzani’s account, the conflict reflected not only local grievances but also the broader Kurdish disappointment that followed the post-war settlement. He links the uprising to the unfulfilled promises of the Treaty of Sèvres, the incorporation of Mosul into Iraq, and British influence over the new state. In this telling, Barzan became one of the places where Kurdish resistance to the new regional order found armed expression.

Yet even Masoud Barzani emphasizes that Sheikh Ahmed did not seek confrontation. He portrays the sheikh as a cautious leader who repeatedly attempted to avoid war and pursued negotiations whenever possible. According to his account, the government demanded the surrender of weapons, the stationing of military forces in Barzan, and the submission of Sheikh Ahmed himself to state authority. The sheikh regarded these conditions as unacceptable because they would effectively dismantle the authority of the Barzan community.

When fighting finally broke out in 1931, the balance of power overwhelmingly favored Baghdad. Iraqi forces enjoyed superior numbers, better equipment, and the support of British military resources. Most importantly, they possessed aircraft. Royal Air Force operations became a defining feature of the campaign, introducing a form of warfare that few Kurdish communities had previously experienced.

McDowall notes that aerial bombardment destroyed 1,365 out of 2,382 dwellings across seventy-nine villages around Barzan. The war became an early example of the disproportionate force that modern states could deploy against remote mountain communities.

By 1932, the revolt had been crushed. Facing overwhelming military pressure, Sheikh Ahmed Barzani and several other leaders fled across the border into Turkey, only to be handed over to the Iraqi authorities. Determined to prevent the resurgence of Barzan's influence, Baghdad exiled Sheikh Ahmed and his younger brother Mustafa Barzani, the future dominant leader of the Barzanis, to southern Iraq and later to Slemani (Sulaimaniyah), placing them under close surveillance and separating them from the communities that had long sustained their authority. The defeat marked the beginning of a difficult decade for the Barzani movement, as the government sought not only to suppress armed resistance but also to dismantle the social foundations that had made Barzan a powerful force in the region.

Sheikh Ahmed remained the symbolic and spiritual center of the movement throughout these years. Even in exile, he retained the loyalty of his followers and continued to embody the values that had defined Barzan for generations. Abdul Musawwar emphasizes that the movement survived precisely because it was more than a tribe or an armed group. He believes that Barzan functioned as a distinct social order characterized by strong internal solidarity, communal obligations, and a remarkable absence of the feudal relationships that dominated many neighboring areas.

This period illustrates the gradual divergence between Sheikh Ahmed and Mustafa Barzani without creating a division between them. Sheikh Ahmed remained rooted in the religious and communal traditions of Barzan, viewing politics largely through the lens of protecting the community and preserving its autonomy. Mustafa, meanwhile, was beginning to encounter broader currents of Kurdish political thought that connected local struggles to a wider national cause.

Throughout the 1930s, despite exile and periodic restrictions, the Barzanis endured. Baghdad had succeeded in defeating the revolt, but it had failed to destroy the social bonds that held Barzan together. The conditions that had produced conflict in the first place remained unresolved. As the decade progressed and the political landscape of the Middle East shifted once again, those tensions would eventually resurface.

The outbreak of the Second World War transformed political life throughout the Middle East. In Iraq, political instability and foreign intervention weakened the authority of the central government and created new opportunities for opposition movements. The Barzanis gradually found space to rebuild their influence.

The events that led to the Second Barzan Revolt began with the return of Mustafa Barzani to his homeland in 1943. By this stage, Mustafa had developed into an increasingly respected military figure, while Sheikh Ahmed remained the supreme religious and moral authority of the movement. Their relationship reflected a balance that would define the Barzanis for years to come: Sheikh Ahmed provided spiritual legitimacy and communal leadership, while Mustafa increasingly assumed responsibility for military and political affairs.

The causes of the new conflict remain a matter of historical debate. McDowall argues that the revolt emerged largely from the Iraqi government's treatment of Sheikh Ahmed and Mustafa Barzani rather than from a coherent nationalist programme. According to his reading, the Barzanis initially sought permission simply to live peacefully in their homeland after years of exile and restriction. McDowall points to Mustafa's petitions to the government and his communications with British officials as evidence that the movement's immediate objectives remained local rather than national. He famously cites Mustafa's statement to the British ambassador that he would obey British instructions "as a child would the orders of a compassionate father," observing that such language was “hardly characteristic of a committed nationalist revolutionary”.

Assasard reaches a broadly similar conclusion, although from a different perspective. He argues that Sheikh Ahmed's concerns remained centered on preserving Barzan's religious traditions, social structures, and local autonomy. Resistance to Baghdad reflected fears that government intervention would undermine the communal order that had developed under the Barzani sheikhs over generations. The conflict, therefore, remained fundamentally defensive in nature.

Masoud Barzani offers a markedly different interpretation. In his book, he portrays the second revolt as a more politically mature and nationally conscious movement than the first revolt. While acknowledging that local grievances remained important, he argues that the movement had evolved significantly during the intervening years. He states that during that time the Barzanis had established connections with Kurdish officers within Iraqi state army, nationalist circles, and political organizations across Kurdistan.

What is not disputed is the growing importance of Mustafa Barzani. As fighting resumed, he demonstrated increasingly sophisticated military leadership. Government forces repeatedly found themselves frustrated by Barzani tactics, ambushes, and maneuvering in difficult mountain terrain. McDowall notes that Mustafa's military successes significantly enhanced his reputation, while Masoud Barzani presents these victories as a turning point not only for the Barzanis but for the wider Kurdish movement.

As the conflict intensified, the Iraqi government committed greater military resources to the campaign. Despite a series of tactical successes by Barzani fighters, once again the balance of power remained overwhelmingly in Baghdad's favor.

By late 1945 and early 1946, it was becoming clear that the movement faced a familiar dilemma. Continued resistance risked the destruction of Barzan and the loss of much of its remaining strength. Surrender offered little guarantee of security given the experiences of previous decades. Faced with these realities, the Barzani leadership began to look beyond Iraq's borders.

The decision they eventually made would have a lasting impact on the course of Kurdish history. In the spring of 1946, Sheikh Ahmed, Mustafa Barzani, and hundreds of their followers crossed into Iran. Behind them lay decades of struggle, exile, and confrontation with successive governments. Ahead of them lay a new political experiment that was capturing the imagination of Kurds throughout the region: the Republic of Kurdistan in Mahabad, Iran.

When the Barzanis crossed into Iranian Kurdistan, they entered a world profoundly different from the one that had shaped them for generations. The mountains of Barzan had produced a movement rooted in religious authority and the defense of local autonomy. Mahabad, by contrast, represented something new in Kurdish history: an explicitly political project that sought to transform Kurdish aspirations into the institutions of a modern state.

The Republic of Kurdistan in Mahabad emerged from the extraordinary circumstances created by the Second World War. Following the Allied occupation of Iran in 1941, Soviet forces established control over large parts of the country's northwestern regions. The weakening of Tehran's authority created political opportunities that Kurdish activists had never previously enjoyed. Under the leadership of Qazi Muhammad, a respected judge, religious figure, and political leader from Mahabad, Kurdish intellectuals and local notables sought to construct institutions capable of representing Kurdish interests within this unusual geopolitical environment.

Despite the enormous symbolic significance of the republic, its practical weaknesses were immediately apparent. Mahabad possessed political leadership, a growing administrative structure, and considerable enthusiasm among many Kurdish communities, but it lacked a military force capable of defending itself against Tehran's inevitable efforts to restore control. It was precisely at this moment that the arrival of Mustafa Barzani and his followers helped to transform the republic's prospects. Having fought successive campaigns against the Iraqi state, the Barzanis brought with them experienced fighters, military discipline, and a reputation that extended across much of Kurdistan.

Masoud Barzani presents this development as a historic convergence between two currents within Kurdish history. According to him, the decision to move into Iranian Kurdistan was not simply an act of refuge undertaken by a defeated force. Rather, it was a deliberate strategy intended to preserve what he calls a "tested force" and place it at the service of a wider Kurdish cause. In this telling, the Barzanis arrived not as guests but as participants in a national project whose success required both political leadership and military strength. The republic gained a capable army, while the Barzanis found themselves incorporated into a movement that extended far beyond the valleys and villages of their homeland.

The optimism surrounding Mahabad proved short-lived. Dependent on Soviet support, the republic became increasingly vulnerable after Soviet forces withdrew from northern Iran. As Tehran swiftly reasserted its authority, the republic collapsed in December 1946.

The collapse forced the Barzanis into yet another difficult circumstance. Iranian authorities sought to neutralize the remaining armed forces, while the Iraqi government closely monitored developments across the border. The possibility of maintaining a large independent military force inside Iranian rapidly disappeared. According to Assasard, Iranian officials at one stage explored the possibility of settling the Barzanis permanently within Iran, offering land and relative security if they abandoned their military mobility. Mustafa Barzani reportedly considered the proposal as a practical means of protecting his people after the fall of the republic. Sheikh Ahmed, however, strongly opposed such a course. For him, Barzan was not merely a homeland but the spiritual center of the community's identity. Exile, however comfortable, could never replace it.

to Masoud Barzani's account, a strategic decision gradually took shape whereby Sheikh Ahmed would accompany much of the civilian population while Mustafa would remain with a smaller armed force capable of maneuvering independently. This arrangement reflected the different roles the two brothers had come to embody.

When Sheikh Ahmed returned toward Iraq with many of the families associated with the movement, he did so knowing that imprisonment was a likely outcome. Upon his return, he and several members of the Barzani family were detained. Mustafa Barzani, however, chose a course that few believed possible. Gathering several hundred fighters, he began a long and perilous journey northward toward the Soviet Union.
Shiekh Ahmed and Mustafa Barzani, Baghdad, 1958

McDowall describes the journey as one of the defining episodes in the creation of the Barzani legend. Pursued by Iranian forces and operating in extremely difficult terrain, Mustafa's group crossed mountains, rivers, and hostile territory under constant pressure to reach the Soviet Union. For the first time, the movement's principal military leader found himself beyond the boundaries of the Middle East and under the authority of a global superpower.

The Barzanis were initially treated with caution in the Soviet Union, as officials weighed regional considerations and their potential political value. Conditions later improved, giving Mustafa Barzani and his followers access to education and new forms of political and organizational experience. Although Barzani never embraced communism, his years in exile broadened his political outlook and influenced the later development of the Kurdish movement.

Indeed, in a letter sent from the Soviet Union in 1955, Mustafa Barzani asked the Kurdish intellectual and translator Rahimi Qazi to find someone capable of translating the works of Karl Marx and Vladimir Lenin into Kurdish.

While Mustafa remained in exile, important developments were taking place among Kurdish intellectuals and activists inside Iraq. The Kurdish political landscape of the 1940s differed markedly from the world in which the Barzan movement had first emerged. Urban centers such as Slemani, Kirkuk, and Baghdad had produced a generation of teachers, civil servants, journalists, students, and former officers who sought new methods for advancing Kurdish interests. The Iraqi Communist Party had considerable support among the Kurds. Organizations such as Hiwa had already demonstrated the growing appeal of modern political activism, but many activists remained frustrated by the absence of a unified national organization capable of mobilizing Kurdish society on a larger scale.

The eventual creation of the Kurdistan Democratic Party (KDP) reflected an effort to bridge that gap. Historians such as Chris Kutschera have argued that Kurdish intellectuals increasingly recognized the limitations of operating without a widely respected popular leader. They possessed political programs, organizational skills, and ideological frameworks, but they lacked a figure capable of commanding loyalty across tribal and social divisions. Mustafa Barzani, although absent in Soviet exile, possessed precisely that kind of legitimacy. As a result, when the KDP emerged in 1946 and developed during the following years, Mustafa increasingly became the symbolic figure around whom many Kurdish activists sought to unite.

The political landscape shifted dramatically once again on 14 July 1958, when a group of Iraqi officers led by Abd al-Karim Qasim overthrew the monarchy. This ended the Hashemite regime, which had governed Iraq since its creation, and opened a new chapter in relations between Baghdad and the Kurds. For the Barzanis, the consequences were immediate and profound. Sheikh Ahmed regained his freedom after eleven years of imprisonment and Mustafa Barzani returned from Soviet exile.

Initially, relations between Mustafa Barzani and Qasim appeared promising. Qasim publicly acknowledged Kurdish national rights and spoke of Arabs and Kurds as partners in the Iraqi state. Mustafa responded positively, hoping that a new era of cooperation might be possible. For a brief period, many observers believed that the longstanding conflict between Baghdad and the Kurdish movement could finally be resolved through political accommodation.

The optimism proved short-lived. Although Qasim recognized certain Kurdish cultural rights, deeper disputes soon emerged over authority in the Barzan region and the future balance of power in Iraqi Kurdistan. Mustafa Barzani sought to re-establish the influence that the Barzanis had exercised before their years of exile, a development that alarmed neighboring tribes, particularly the Zebaris, who feared the return of Barzani dominance. According to Jalal Talabani's account, clashes between Barzani and Zebari fighters resulted in several deaths, prompting Qasim to arm the Zebaris for their own protection and further deepening mistrust between Baghdad and the Barzanis.

At the same time, Qasim's ambitious land reform program unsettled much of the Kurdish tribal elite by threatening traditional patterns of landownership and authority. Many historians argue that this became one of the principal catalysts of the 1961 revolt. Mustafa Barzani capitalized on the widespread opposition to the reforms, bringing together tribal leaders, landlords, and other dissatisfied groups under a common front against Baghdad. Nationalist rhetoric undoubtedly played an important role in mobilizing support, but the uprising was driven as much by local grievances, tribal interests, and resistance to state intervention as by the pursuit of Kurdish national rights. By 1961, the fragile partnership between Qasim and the Barzanis had collapsed, setting the stage for the September Revolution, a conflict that would transform the Kurdish question and firmly establish Mustafa Barzani as the central figure of the movement.

The outbreak of the what is became known as “September Revolution” in 1961 transformed the Kurdish movement in Iraq and fundamentally altered the balance of power within the KDP. What began as a confrontation between Kurdish forces and the government of Abd al-Karim Qasim quickly evolved into the largest Kurdish uprising Iraq had yet witnessed. By the early 1960s, the movement become an organized national struggle that combined political institutions, armed forces, intellectual activists, tribal networks, and a growing sense of Kurdish national identity. Yet the very success of the revolution also exposed tensions that had been developing within the KDP since Mustafa Barzani's return from Soviet exile in 1958.

At the heart of these tensions was a fundamental question: who should lead the Kurdish national movement, and through what mechanisms should that leadership be exercised? The dispute eventually became associated with the rivalry between the conservative Mustafa Barzani on one side and the progressives Ibrahim Ahmed and Jalal Talabani on the other. However, the conflict was never merely a personal disagreement. It reflected competing visions of authority, organization, and political legitimacy within a rapidly evolving national movement.

The roots of the dispute stretched back to the origins of the KDP itself. When the party was founded in Baghdad in August 1946, Mustafa Barzani was not present. He was deeply involved in the events surrounding the Republic of Mahabad and would soon enter exile in the Soviet Union. Nevertheless, his reputation among Iraqi Kurds was already considerable. For that reason, the founders of the party elected him president despite his absence in the hope of having a prominent figure with ceremonial powers. At the time, however, the position was largely symbolic. The daily affairs of the organization were managed by party intellectuals, activists, and organizers operating inside Iraq.

During Mustafa's eleven years in Soviet exile, the party developed largely under the leadership of Ibrahim Ahmed, who emerged as one of the most influential Kurdish intellectuals of the period. A lawyer, writer, and political organizer, Ibrahim played a central role in building the party's organizational structure, developing its political programme, and expanding its influence among students, teachers, civil servants, and urban activists. Around him emerged a younger generation of politically active Kurdish nationalists, among whom Jalal Talabani (who becomes Ibrahim Ahmed’s son-in-law in 1970) quickly became one of the most talented and ambitious figures. Together, they represented a political current that sought to modernize Kurdish politics through stronger institutions, collective leadership, ideological development, and a more structured party apparatus.

The balance that had existed during Mustafa's absence changed dramatically after his return to Iraq in 1958. He returned not as a ceremonial president but as a legendary figure whose reputation had only grown during years of exile. More importantly, he returned with a loyal following, close ties to the Kurdish fighters known as Peshmerga forces, and immense prestige among rural Kurdish communities. While Ibrahim Ahmed and his allies exercised authority through party institutions, Mustafa possessed something equally important: tribal and popular support.

Most Kurdish and non-Kurdish historians who have written about this period broadly agree on the central source of the conflict. McDowall, Assasard, Ayoub Barzani, and numerous other writers differ on many aspects of Kurdish history, but they generally concur that the dispute emerged because Mustafa Barzani was unwilling to remain merely a symbolic president subject to the authority of the party apparatus. During his years in exile, the KDP's leadership had become accustomed to operating independently through its Political Bureau, Central Committee, and secretary-general. After 1958, Mustafa increasingly asserted his own authority over major political and military decisions, often bypassing or overshadowing these institutions. For many members of the party leadership, this represented a challenge to the organizational principles upon which they believed the KDP should function.

The outbreak of the September Revolution intensified these tensions. Once armed conflict with Baghdad began, military considerations increasingly dominated Kurdish politics. As commander of the Peshmerga and leader of the armed movement, Mustafa's influence naturally expanded. Strategic decisions often had to be made quickly, and wartime conditions tended to favor centralized leadership rather than lengthy institutional deliberation. Mustafa's supporters argued that a revolutionary movement engaged in armed struggle could not be effectively directed by distant party officials who lacked direct responsibility for military operations. Ibrahim Ahmed, Talabani, and their allies countered that even during wartime, major decisions should remain subject to party oversight and collective leadership.

The dispute reached a critical stage in 1964 following negotiations with the government of Abdul Salam Arif. Mustafa Barzani accepted a ceasefire that he regarded as a necessary tactical pause after years of fighting, destruction, and displacement. He believed the movement needed time to regroup, rebuild its strength, and relieve pressure on Kurdish civilians. His opponents disagreed. Ibrahim Ahmed and Jalal Talabani argued not only that the agreement offered insufficient guarantees for Kurdish rights but also that Mustafa had reached it without proper consultation with the party leadership. For them, the issue was not simply the content of the ceasefire but the process through which it had been negotiated. They viewed it as evidence that Mustafa increasingly considered himself above the institutions of the party.

The disagreement soon escalated into a full-scale organizational crisis. Mustafa and his supporters convened a party congress in July 1964 that reaffirmed his leadership and removed many of the figures associated with the old Political Bureau. Ibrahim Ahmed, Talabani, and their allies rejected the congress as illegitimate and accused Mustafa of using his military influence to dominate the party. Mustafa's camp responded that the Political Bureau faction had become disconnected from the realities of the revolution and was attempting to undermine the recognized leader of the Kurdish struggle.

At this point, the interpretations of historians begin to diverge. Most scholars, including McDowall, Assasard, and Ayoub Barzani, emphasize the extent to which Mustafa increasingly overshadowed party institutions and concentrated authority in his own hands. They generally portray the dispute as a struggle over whether the KDP would function as a modern political party or become subordinate to the authority of a single leader. Masoud Barzani, however, presents the events very differently in his account of the Kurdish movement.

In his memoir, writing as a living witnesser and son, Masoud Barzani offers a narrative that is unmistakably sympathetic to his father. While acknowledging the existence of disagreements, he portrays Mustafa Barzani as the historically legitimate leader of the Kurdish national struggle whose authority rested upon decades of sacrifice, resistance, and popular support. In Masoud Barzani's telling, Ibrahim Ahmed and Jalal Talabani appear less as defenders of party institutions and more as leaders of a faction attempting to challenge an authority that had already been recognized by the Kurdish people. He repeatedly emphasizes that the overwhelming majority of Peshmerga fighters, rural communities, and ordinary supporters remained loyal to Mustafa.

The split eventually extended beyond politics and into armed confrontation. Mustafa Barzani decided to attack the faction and clashed with supporters of Ibrahim Ahmed, while the Iraqi government actively sought to exploit the divisions for its own benefit. The opposition faction eventually cooperated with Baghdad against Mustafa's forces, a decision that allowed Barzani's supporters to portray them as ‘collaborators’. Although temporary reconciliations would occur in later years, the underlying dispute remained unresolved ever since.

The consequences of this conflict would extend far beyond the 1960s. Many of the divisions that later shaped Kurdish politics, including the eventual emergence of the PUK under Jalal Talabani, can be traced to these disputes. What began as a disagreement over leadership within a single party ultimately evolved into one of the defining political fault lines of modern Kurdish history.
The Death of Sheikh Ahmed and the End of the Barzan Sheikhdom

Returning to the spiritual lineage of the Barzanis, Sheikh Ahmed remained the most revered religious figure in Barzan after his release from prison in 1958. Yet his relationship with the movement's evolving political reality was not always straightforward. According to Assasard, tensions gradually emerged between Sheikh Ahmed and Mustafa Barzani following the outbreak of the September Revolution. By then, Sheikh Ahmed had largely withdrawn from political and military affairs. Mustafa, meanwhile, had become the undisputed military and political leader of the Kurdish national movement, transforming Barzan into the operational center of a growing armed struggle against Baghdad. Assasard notes that Sheikh Ahmed at one point even asked Mustafa to move his activities away from Barzan, reflecting his concern that the region's religious character was being overshadowed by war and politics. Although the disagreement never severed the bond between the two brothers, it symbolized a deeper historical transition: the gradual separation of the old Barzan sheikhdom from the modern nationalist movement that had come to be embodied by Mustafa Barzani.

When Sheikh Ahmed died in 1969, that transition accelerated. His death removed the last spiritual leader of the Barzan community and immediately raised the question of succession. Many within Barzan believed that Sheikh Ahmed had intended his nephew, Khurshid Barzani, to inherit his religious position and continue the sheikhly tradition. Yet he had never formally designated a successor. The ambiguity opened the door to competing claims, particularly from Sheikh Ahmed's sons, who argued that leadership should remain within his direct family line. The most prominent challenger was Sheikh Osman, who asserted his own claim to the sheikhdom and, during the ensuing dispute, expelled Khurshid from Barzan. As tensions deepened, Mustafa Barzani intervened decisively and threw his support behind Muhammad Khalid, the eldest son of Sheikh Ahmed, rather than Osman, effectively determining the outcome of the succession struggle. Nevertheless, Muhammad Khalid never gained sufficient support and respect to become his father's undisputed successor.

The conflict carried consequences that extended far beyond a family disagreement. Sheikh Osman eventually aligned himself with the Iraqi government, creating a lasting division within the wider Barzani family. Although relations between different branches of the family improved at various points in subsequent decades, the rupture never disappeared entirely and later resurfaced in political disputes involving Sheikh Osman's son, Adham Barzani. More significantly, the succession crisis revealed that the institutional foundations of the Barzan sheikhdom had weakened beyond repair. Unlike earlier generations, no successor emerged with the authority, prestige, or broad recognition necessary to unite the community around a single spiritual leader. While some followers continued to regard Khurshid Barzani as Sheikh Ahmed's intended heir and maintained loyalty to him as a religious figure, his influence remained limited and ended with his death during the 1983 Barzani genocide.

In historical terms, Sheikh Ahmed's death effectively marked the end of the Barzan sheikhdom as a living institution. For nearly two centuries, leadership in Barzan had rested primarily on spiritual authority exercised through the line of the sheikhs. After 1969, that authority passed decisively into the political sphere. The center of gravity within the Barzani movement shifted from the takya and the sheikhly household to the KDP, the Peshmerga, and the nationalist leadership of Mustafa Barzani.
The End of the September Revolution and the Barzanis in Exile, Again

After almost a decade of insurgency, the Kurdish movement under the leadership of Mustafa Barzani and the Iraqi government reached a comprehensive agreement. March 11, 1970, known as the March Agreement, marked the closest the Kurdish movement and the Iraqi state had come to a political settlement since the creation of modern Iraq. After nearly a decade of war, neither side had achieved a decisive victory. The government in Baghdad faced the mounting economic and military costs of a conflict that had proven impossible to suppress through force alone, while the Kurdish leadership understood that even the most successful guerrilla movement could not indefinitely substitute for a durable political arrangement. The agreement emerged from this mutual reality. It was less a product of trust than of necessity.

For Mustafa Barzani, the accord appeared to validate decades of struggle. Since his return from Soviet, he had transformed the KDP from a relatively small political organization into the dominant force in Iraqi Kurdistan. The Iraqi state's formal recognition of Kurdish national rights seemed to confirm that armed resistance had succeeded where petitions, negotiations, and appeals had repeatedly failed. Kurdish-language education expanded, cultural institutions flourished, and for the first time the prospect of a constitutionally recognized Kurdish autonomous region appeared attainable.

Yet beneath the optimism of the agreement, fundamental disagreements remained unresolved, particularly over Kirkuk, which the Kurdish leadership regarded as an essential part of Kurdistan while Baghdad considered strategically indispensable because of its oil wealth. Although both sides publicly spoke of partnership and reconciliation, mutual suspicion persisted. Kurdish officials accused the government of delaying implementation of the agreement, manipulating census results, and continuing Arabization policies in disputed territories, while Baghdad remained wary of Kurdish diplomatic activity and military preparations. Whatever trust still existed suffered a severe blow in September 1971, when an Iraqi attempt to assassinate Mustafa Barzani failed. The incident reinforced Kurdish fears that the government remained committed to eliminating its leadership even while publicly negotiating autonomy.

The years following the Autonomy Agreement brought the Kurdish movement to the height of its political influence, but they also forced its leadership to confront a sensitive question that could no longer be postponed: succession. Mustafa Barzani had emerged from the September Revolution as the undisputed leader of Iraqi Kurdistan. By the early 1970s, however, he was approaching seventy years of age. Although he remained active and energetic, both his supporters and his opponents understood that the future of the movement would eventually depend on who would inherit the responsibilities he had carried for so long.
Unlike many nationalist leaders who avoided the subject altogether, Mustafa appears to have approached the issue pragmatically.

Mustafa Barzani had ten sons and several daughters from three wives: two from the Barzani family, Mahbuba and Hafsa, and a third, Hamayl, from the Zebari tribe. The marriage to Hamayl is often viewed as a successful effort to ease tensions and strengthen relations between the two communities. A number of historians have suggested that Hamayl was Mustafa Barzani's favorite wife. Nearly three decades younger than him, she is also said to have exercised considerable influence within the family, particularly on matters relating to succession and the future leadership of the movement. Masoud Barzani, who would later become the most prominent political heir to his father, was the eldest son born from this marriage.

Throughout the 1960s and early 1970s Mustafa increasingly entrusted important responsibilities to two of his sons, Idris and Masoud. Their rise was gradual rather than sudden. Neither was formally designated as a successor, yet both steadily accumulated experience in areas that would prove essential to the future of the Kurdish movement.

Idris, a son of Mustafa Barzani's second wife, Hafsa, was regarded as the more prominent political figure during this period. Fluent, diplomatic, and politically astute, he became one of the principal intermediaries between the Kurdish leadership and foreign governments. He participated in negotiations with Baghdad, maintained relations with regional and international actors, and gradually established himself as one of the most recognizable representatives of the Kurdish cause beyond Kurdistan. Masoud, who was younger and less visible internationally, followed a somewhat different path. He spent much of the period involved in military affairs, intelligence work, internal organization, and the day-to-day management of the movement's growing structures. While still in his early twenties, he was entrusted with leadership of the KDP's powerful intelligence and security organization, Parastin, a position that reflected both his father's confidence in him and his growing importance within the movement.

Ayoub Barzani places particular emphasis on Mustafa's deliberate efforts to prepare both men for future responsibilities. In his account, Mustafa viewed leadership not as a hereditary right but as a burden that required long preparation and practical experience. Rather than elevating his sons immediately to positions of formal authority, he gradually exposed them to different aspects of political and military life. Ayoub describes how Idris and Masoud accompanied their father during negotiations, participated in strategic discussions, interacted with foreign delegations, and learned the complex realities of Kurdish politics from within. According to Ayoub, Mustafa wanted them to understand not only warfare but also diplomacy, administration, and the difficult balance between principle and pragmatism that had characterized his own political career.

All of this unfolded before the eyes of Mustafa Barzani's eldest sons from his first wife, Mahbuba - Ubaydallah, Luqman, and Sabir - who watched as influence and responsibility were increasingly concentrated in the hands of Idris and Masoud, gradually pushing them to the margins of the movement.

The issue attracted the attention of foreign governments as well. A memorandum prepared in July 1972 by General Alexander Haig for Henry Kissinger identified succession as one of the central questions facing the Kurdish movement. The document observed that while Mustafa remained vigorous and politically dominant, “his own years of leadership are limited.” American officials noted that Idris and Masoud had emerged as his principal supporters, with Idris playing a leading role in external relations and Masoud overseeing intelligence and organizational matters. The memorandum questioned whether either son possessed the charisma, prestige, or broad appeal of their father and speculated that future leadership of the Kurdish movement might instead “likely to pass to Jalal Talabani or someone like him”. What particularly concerned American observers was that signs of division were already emerging within the wider Barzani family itself. The same memorandum pointed to the activities of two of Mustafa's older sons, Luqman and Ubaydallah, who had established close relations with the Iraqi government. American officials concluded that the two men had effectively “sold out to the Baghdad government.”

McDowall records the episode as one of the most painful moments in Mustafa Barzani's personal life. Among the defections, the most damaging involved his eldest son, Ubaydallah. According to McDowall, Ubaydallah publicly criticized his father and accused him of deliberately obstructing implementation of the autonomy agreement. In a striking statement, Ubaydallah reportedly claimed that his father "does not want self-rule to be implemented even if he was given Kirkuk and all of its oil. His acceptance of the law will take everything from him, and he wants to remain the absolute ruler." He further condemned his father for failing to implement agrarian reforms that many Kurdish intellectuals considered essential.

Masoud Barzani's account differs significantly in tone and emphasis. While acknowledging the existence of family tensions, he presents his father's actions in a far more sympathetic light. In his narrative, Mustafa's reliance on Idris and Masoud was not motivated by dynastic ambition but by practical necessity. After decades of war and political struggle, Mustafa needed individuals whom he trusted completely and who had demonstrated unwavering commitment to the Kurdish cause. Idris and Masoud, in this interpretation, earned their positions not because they were his sons but because they had spent years serving the movement, sharing its hardships, and proving their capabilities.

Masoud's account also portrays the defections of Luqman and Ubaydallah less as legitimate political disagreements than as tragic personal choices encouraged by Baghdad's efforts to divide the Kurdish movement. Rather than presenting them as independent political actors, he situates their actions within a broader strategy employed by the Iraqi government to weaken the Kurdish leadership through internal fragmentation.

Beyond these disputes, another layer of tension was unfolding within the wider Barzani family. Ayoub Barzani presents the conflict involving Sheikh Osman, a son of Sheikh Ahmed Barzani, as one of the most consequential internal disputes of the period. According to his account, Sheikh Ahmed had maintained a balance among his sons by assigning them distinct responsibilities: Sheikh Muhammad Khalid managed relations with the Iraqi government, while Sheikh Osman oversaw affairs within Barzan itself. Ayoub argues that this arrangement began to unravel when Mustafa Barzani increasingly favored Muhammad Khalid and sought to curtail Osman's independent authority. In his interpretation, Osman represented a branch of the family that still drew legitimacy from the older traditions of the Barzan sheikhdom and remained critical of certain policies pursued by Mustafa. Ayoub contends that Mustafa gradually marginalized Osman through political pressure, patronage networks, and alliances within the family, eventually leaving him with little choice but to seek support from Baghdad. While acknowledging that Osman ultimately established relations with the Iraqi government, Ayoub portrays this less as a deliberate act of collaboration than as the outcome of an internal struggle in which Mustafa sought to eliminate alternative centers of authority within the broader Barzani family.

Ayoub places the later alignment of Luqman and Ubaydallah Barzani with Baghdad within the same broader pattern of family fragmentation. Rather than depicting them simply as opportunistic defectors, he argues that their estrangement reflected growing dissatisfaction with what he describes as Mustafa Barzani's increasingly centralized style of leadership. In Ayoub's view, Baghdad skillfully exploited these divisions by promoting members of the Barzani family as alternatives to Mustafa's authority, thereby weakening his claim to speak on behalf of Barzan as a whole. He links these disputes to what he calls the "destructive project of inheritance," arguing that Mustafa was gradually concentrating political and military power around his own sons, particularly Idris and Masoud, at the expense of other branches descended from Sheikh Ahmed. Although Ayoub acknowledges that the decisions of Luqman and Ubaydallah ultimately benefited Baghdad and damaged Kurdish unity, he maintains that their defections cannot be understood solely as acts of betrayal. Rather, he sees them as the product of exclusion, internal rivalries, and the transformation of the Barzani movement from a broader religious-family network into a more centralized political leadership structure.

The sudden death of Idris Barzani in Iran in 1987, at the age of just 43, profoundly altered the balance of leadership within both the KDP and the wider Barzani movement. With Idris gone, Masoud Barzani emerged as the unchallenged leading figure of the next generation. From that point onward, he consolidated his position as the undisputed leader of the KDP and, increasingly, the most influential figure within the Barzani family as a whole.

By 1974, the March Agreement had effectively collapsed. Baghdad introduced its own autonomy law, but the Kurdish leadership rejected it, arguing that it failed to fulfill the promises made four years earlier. Most importantly, it excluded key disputed territories such as Kirkuk and left substantial powers in the hands of the central government. What Baghdad presented as autonomy, the Kurds viewed as a watered-down version of the original agreement.

The dispute soon escalated into renewed war. Unlike the 1960s, however, the balance of power had shifted. Iraq's armed forces had been significantly strengthened through Soviet military assistance, while the Kurdish movement increasingly depended on support from the Shah of Iran. For the Shah, backing the Kurds was less about Kurdish rights than about using them as leverage against Baghdad in the ongoing dispute over the Shatt al-Arab waterway. Mustafa Barzani understood the risks of relying on foreign support, but he had few alternatives.

The decisive blow came in March 1975, when Iraq and Iran signed the Algiers Agreement. In exchange for concessions from Baghdad on border issues, Iran immediately ended its support for the Kurdish insurgency. Supply lines were cut, military aid disappeared, and Kurdish forces suddenly found themselves isolated. Appeals to the United States and other allies produced no meaningful response. Mahmoud Osman, one of Mustafa Barzani’s closest advisers, said that after the 1975 Algiers Agreement, the Shah informed Kurdish representatives that Iran had reached a deal with Baghdad and that “a third party” had lost, meaning the Kurds. Barzani then concluded that the revolt could no longer continue. He effectively announced the end of the revolution in an interview with Iran’s Kayhan newspaper, using his famous phrase: “My work is finished,” referring to the end of his military and political leadership of the Iraqi Kurdish movement at that moment. Within weeks, the revolution collapsed. Hundreds of thousands of Kurdish civilians fled to Iran, villages were abandoned, and years of struggle came to an abrupt end. For many Kurds, the events of 1975 left a lasting sense of betrayal that would shape Kurdish political memory for decades.
The Struggle for Survival

The collapse of the revolution forced the Barzanis into exile once again. Nearly three decades after the fall of the Republic of Mahabad, they found themselves confronting another devastating defeat. Mustafa Barzani settled in Iran with thousands of followers, but his health was deteriorating, while the Kurdish movement entered a period of profound reorganization. The failure of 1975 deepened divisions within Kurdish politics and contributed to the emergence of the PUK under Jalal Talabani, creating a powerful rival to the KDP.
Mustafa Barzani with Idris and Masoud, Washington 1977

The circumstances were difficult. The Kurdish movement was divided, many supporters remained in exile, and Baghdad believed the Barzanis had been permanently defeated. Yet the Temporary Leadership gradually rebuilt its networks, and by the late 1970s and early 1980s small Peshmerga units had begun returning to the mountains. The 1979 Islamic Revolution in Iran and the subsequent Iran-Iraq War created new opportunities, as the KDP once again found support from a regional power, the Islamic Republic of Iran, in its struggle against Baghdad, a partnership that would ultimately carry heavy consequences for the Barzani community.
The Barzani Genocide of 1983

The genocide of the Barzanis in 1983 represented one of the darkest episodes in modern Kurdish history and marked the culmination of a long confrontation between the Iraqi state and a community that had become synonymous with Kurdish resistance. The immediate backdrop was the Iran-Iraq War and the renewed activities of the KDP under the leadership of Idris and Masoud Barzani. In 1983, the KDP forces cooperated with Iranians in an operation around the Haji Omaran border region, northeast Erbil. The objectives of the two sides were not entirely identical. Iranian forces sought to weaken Iranian Kurdish opposition groups operating along the frontier, while Barzani forces were also motivated by local rivalries, particularly longstanding disputes involving the Bradosti tribe. Although the operation achieved limited military gains, it provided Baghdad with a pretext for a far broader campaign of retaliation.

Of course, as Ayoub Barzani says, however, the roots of the tragedy extended beyond the events at Haji Omaran. The genocide was part of Saddam Hussein's wider system of collective punishment, in which responsibility was deliberately extended beyond individual opponents to encompass their families, relatives, and entire social communities. Within this logic, the Barzanis were targeted not because of the actions of specific fighters but because "Barzani" itself had become a political symbol.

The Iraqi response was swift and ruthless. Thousands of Barzani men and boys were arrested from collective settlements where many families had been confined since the collapse of the September Revolution in 1975. Security forces separated males from their families and transported them to detention centers before transferring them to remote areas in southern Iraq. For years, relatives received no information about their fate. Only after the fall of Saddam Hussein's regime in 2003 did the discovery of mass graves confirm that thousands had been systematically executed. Estimates generally place the number of victims at around 8,000 men and boys.

One of the most striking aspects of the campaign was its indiscriminate nature. The victims included not only KDP supporters but also members of Barzani family branches that had previously opposed Mustafa Barzani or cooperated with Baghdad. Among them was Sheikh Osman, who had been involved in a bitter dispute with Mustafa during the 1970s and later established ties with the Iraqi government, yet he and two of his sons were ultimately killed by Saddam Hussein's regime. The same fate befell other branches of the family, demonstrating that political loyalty offered little protection once the state decided to target the Barzanis collectively. Even figures such as Mustafa Barzani’s sons, Ubaydallah, Sabir and Luqman, who had broken with their father and aligned themselves with Baghdad, were eventually arrested and executed. In a televised statement, Saddam Hussein defended the executions, claiming that the victims had "betrayed" Iraq and had therefore been "sent to hell."

The 1983 Barzani genocide and the wider Anfal campaign of 1987–1989 inflicted devastating losses on both the Barzani community and Kurdistan as a whole. Yet neither succeeded in eliminating Kurdish resistance. As Baghdad's authority weakened following Iraq's defeat in the Gulf War, Kurdish political forces seized a historic opportunity. By the time the uprising of March 1991 erupted, leadership had passed to a new generation. The old Barzan sheikhdom was gone, and the era of Mustafa Barzani had become history, but a modern Kurdish political movement had survived and was poised to help establish de facto Kurdish self-rule in northern Iraq, with the KDP emerging as one of its principal institutions.

This article is based primarily on the sources listed below. Whenever possible, events, dates, and factual claims were cross-checked against multiple works. In cases where authors offer differing interpretations, narratives, or analyses, those disagreements have been identified and discussed in the relevant sections of the article.
Abdul Musawwar Barzani, The Protection of the Kurds (1986).
Ayoub Barzani, Barzan and the Kurdish National Consciousness Movement, 1826–1914. (1980)
Ayoub Barzani, The Kurdish Liberation Movement and the Rivalry of Regional and International Powers, 1958–1975. (Editions Orient-Réalités, Genève, 2011).
Chris Kutschera, Le Mouvement National Kurde (Paris: Flammarion, 1979). (Consulted through the Kurdish translation.)
David McDowall, A Modern History of the Kurds (London: I.B. Tauris, 1996; revised editions 1997, 2004, and 2021).
Didar Osman, History of Barzan from Sheikh Abdul Salam I to the End of Sheikh Abdul Salam II in the Light of Ottoman State Documents. (2021).
Farid Assasard, The Origins of Barzani Belief (Kurdistan Center for Strategic Studies, 2012).
Masoud Barzani, Barzani and the Kurdish Liberation Movement (all volumes, 1997).
